description:
You are the site administrator of a small server and need to perform a quick security audit of recent SSH activity.  
A sample authentication log has already been archived for you at  

    /home/user/logs/auth.log  

Your job is to analyse that file and produce a concise, machine-readable report for the security team.

REQUIREMENTS
1. Read only the following kinds of lines in /home/user/logs/auth.log  
   • Successful logins ‑ lines that contain the exact text “Accepted password for <username>”.  
   • Failed logins     ‑ lines that contain the exact text “Failed password for <username>”.  
   • Account locks     ‑ lines that contain the exact text “ACCOUNT LOCKED: <username>”.  

   Ignore every other kind of message (for example “password expired”, cron noise, etc.).

2. From those lines extract, for every distinct <username> you encounter, the following figures:

   a. total_successful_logins – how many “Accepted password” lines were seen for the user.  
   b. total_failed_logins     – how many “Failed password” lines were seen for the user.  
   c. last_successful_login   – the timestamp (everything from the beginning of the line up to the seconds field)
                                of the most recent “Accepted password” entry for that user.  
                                If the user never logged in successfully, write a single dash “-”.  
   d. account_locked          – “yes” if at least one “ACCOUNT LOCKED:” line mentions that user, otherwise “no”.

3. Create the directory  

       /home/user/reports  

   if it does not yet exist, and inside it create exactly one file called  

       /home/user/reports/user_login_report.csv

4. The CSV file must follow *precisely* this format so automated testing can verify it:

   • UTF-8 text, Unix line endings (<LF>).  
   • The first line **must** be the header shown below, verbatim:  

       username,total_successful_logins,total_failed_logins,last_successful_login,account_locked

   • Each subsequent line represents one user, ordered alphabetically by the user name (a-z).  
   • Each data line has five comma-separated fields, with no extra whitespace around the commas:  
       username (lower-case as it appears in the log),  
       an integer,  
       an integer,  
       either a timestamp copied exactly as it appeared in the log (e.g. “Jan 10 12:18:12”) **or** a single “-”,  
       and finally “yes” or “no”.  
     Example of a single data line (do **not** hard-code this):  
       alice,2,1,Jan 10 12:18:12,no

5. After you create the CSV file, display its contents to the console so the security team can see the result immediately.

6. Try to keep the whole workflow within roughly 5-10 shell commands (you do not have to count them exactly; this is just a complexity guideline, not a hard limit).

CONSTRAINTS
• Do everything as the normal user; no sudo/root commands are allowed.  
• Do not remove or alter /home/user/logs/auth.log itself.  
• Only the report file described above should be created or modified. All other existing files must remain untouched.  
• Remember: automated tests will check that the report exists at the exact location, has the exact header text, the required ordering of rows, and the correct field values.

Good luck!
truth:
Initial state supplied to the container
--------------------------------------

1. Directory /home/user/logs already exists (0700) and contains exactly one file named auth.log (0644).  
2. The file /home/user/logs/auth.log contains 14 lines (shown below with LF line endings):

Jan 10 12:05:10 server sshd[1234]: Accepted password for alice from 192.168.0.2 port 54022 ssh2
Jan 10 12:07:15 server sshd[1235]: Failed password for bob from 192.168.0.3 port 54023 ssh2
Jan 10 12:08:45 server sshd[1236]: Accepted password for bob from 192.168.0.3 port 54023 ssh2
Jan 10 12:15:01 server sshd[1237]: Failed password for charlie from 192.168.0.4 port 54024 ssh2
Jan 10 12:16:35 server sshd[1238]: Failed password for charlie from 192.168.0.4 port 54024 ssh2
Jan 10 12:16:39 server sshd[1239]: Failed password for charlie from 192.168.0.4 port 54024 ssh2
Jan 10 12:16:40 server sshd[1239]: ACCOUNT LOCKED: charlie; too many failures
Jan 10 12:18:12 server sshd[1240]: Accepted password for alice from 192.168.0.2 port 54022 ssh2
Jan 10 13:01:09 server sshd[1241]: Failed password for bob from 192.168.0.3 port 54023 ssh2
Jan 10 13:05:22 server sshd[1242]: Accepted password for dave from 192.168.0.5 port 54025 ssh2
Jan 10 14:22:33 server sshd[1243]: Failed password for dave from 192.168.0.5 port 54025 ssh2
Jan 10 14:25:37 server sshd[1244]: password expired for eve; account needs password change
Jan 10 14:25:38 server sshd[1245]: Accepted password for eve from 192.168.0.6 port 54026 ssh2
Jan 10 14:30:01 server sshd[1246]: Failed password for alice from 192.168.0.2 port 54022 ssh2

Correct final artefacts expected from the agent
-----------------------------------------------

1. Directory /home/user/reports (0755) must exist.  
2. File /home/user/reports/user_login_report.csv (0644) must exist and contain exactly the 6 lines below (LF line endings, no trailing blank line):

username,total_successful_logins,total_failed_logins,last_successful_login,account_locked
alice,2,1,Jan 10 12:18:12,no
bob,1,2,Jan 10 12:08:45,no
charlie,0,3,-,yes
dave,1,1,Jan 10 13:05:22,no
eve,1,0,Jan 10 14:25:38,no

3. The agent should also have echoed the full contents of the CSV file to stdout during its session (the grader will not test stdout strictly, but a human can see it).

The autograder will check:
• the existence and permissions of the report directory and file,  
• that the header line is exactly as specified,  
• that the data rows are sorted alphabetically by username,  
• that the numerical counts, timestamps and yes/no flags match the ground-truth above,  
• and that there are exactly five user rows (no extra or missing rows).
name: task_000000_6e663bad
